text:
GASTROINTESTINAL ONCOLOGY / PANCREATIC TUMOR BOARD FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Medical Oncology — Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy Completion Visit and Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Review

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Medical Oncologist, GI Oncology]
PROVIDER TYPE: Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Review / Established Patient Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mrs. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman of Ghanaian descent with borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the pancreatic head and uncinate process who presents today following completion of 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy (6 cycles modified FOLFIRINOX followed by 2 cycles FOLFIRI backbone due to oxaliplatin discontinuation for grade 3 neuropathy) for multidisciplinary tumor board review and planning of next treatment steps.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mrs. Osei-Bonsu was initially diagnosed with borderline resectable PDAC approximately 9 months ago when she presented to her primary care physician with a 10-week history of painless progressive jaundice, 14-pound weight loss, new-onset epigastric discomfort radiating to the back, pale greasy stools, pruritus, and dark urine. Physical examination at that time revealed scleral icterus, mild hepatomegaly, and a positive Courvoisier sign. Initial labs showed a total bilirubin of 8.2 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 6.9 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L, CA 19-9 1,204 U/mL, and CEA 7.1 ng/mL.

Pancreatic protocol CT and subsequent MRI/MRCP demonstrated a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head with upstream pancreatic duct dilation to 7 mm, common bile duct dilation to 16 mm, SMA abutment over approximately 170-180 degrees, and focal SMV narrowing without occlusion. No hepatic or peritoneal metastases were identified at baseline. Two sub-centimeter peripancreatic lymph nodes were noted. EUS-guided fine needle biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (CK7+, CK19+, MUC1+, CK20-, CDX2-; Ki-67 ~35%). She was staged cT3 N1 M0, Stage IIB (AJCC 8th edition), borderline resectable by AHPBA/SSO/SSAT criteria.

She was discussed at the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board at diagnosis, and the decision was made to pursue neoadjuvant systemic therapy followed by reassessment for chemoradiation and surgery. Prior to chemotherapy initiation, she underwent uncomplicated ERCP with placement of a fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent (FCSEMS), with bilirubin declining to 1.8 mg/dL within two weeks.

She then initiated modified FOLFIRINOX: oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 (dose-reduced from standard 180 mg/m2 at the outset given concern for biliary stent status and Gilbert syndrome), leucovorin 400 mg/m2, and 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 as 46-hour continuous infusion, every 14 days, with Neulasta (pegfilgrastim) prophylaxis each cycle.

During cycles 1-3, she developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy affecting the fingertips and toes, grade 2 fatigue, grade 1 nausea controlled with ondansetron and prochlorperazine, and one episode of grade 2 diarrhea managed with loperamide and a brief dose hold. CA 19-9 after cycle 2 had declined to 580 U/mL. A restaging CT after 4 cycles demonstrated the primary mass had decreased to 3.0 cm (from 3.8 cm), with SMA abutment reduced to approximately 120 degrees and improvement in SMV narrowing. CA 19-9 at that point was 310 U/mL. The decision was made to continue for a total of 8 cycles.

Unfortunately, during cycles 5-8, her peripheral neuropathy progressed from grade 2 to grade 3, becoming functionally limiting (difficulty buttoning clothing, unsteadiness on uneven surfaces), and oxaliplatin was discontinued after cycle 6. She completed cycles 7 and 8 on the FOLFIRI backbone (fluorouracil, leucovorin, irinotecan only). During this phase, she also lost an additional 6 pounds and experienced persistent grade 2 fatigue, though no hospitalizations were required.

A restaging CT obtained approximately 3 weeks ago, after completion of all 8 cycles, demonstrated the primary pancreatic head mass measuring 2.6 cm (down from 3.8 cm at baseline, representing a 32% reduction in maximum diameter), with SMA abutment estimated at less than 90 degrees. The SMV appeared patent without significant narrowing. No evidence of hepatic or peritoneal metastases was identified. CA 19-9 at completion of chemotherapy was 85 U/mL, representing a 93% decline from the pre-treatment peak of 1,204 U/mL.

This case was reviewed in today's multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board. Present at the tumor board were surgical oncology (hepatobiliary), radiation oncology, interventional radiology, diagnostic radiology, pathology, and medical oncology. The imaging, tumor biology, pathologic confirmation, and the patient's current clinical status were reviewed in detail. The board discussion and consensus recommendation are summarized under Assessment/Plan below.

The patient has been accompanied today by her daughter. She is overall doing better compared to her status mid-chemotherapy. She has noted some recovery in energy. The peripheral neuropathy has improved somewhat with the 4-week treatment break between completing cycle 8 and today's visit, and she estimates it has decreased from grade 3 to approximately grade 1 to low-grade 2.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue has improved compared to the nadir during cycle 7-8. She has not lost additional weight over the past 3 weeks. Appetite has modestly improved. Denies fevers, chills, or night sweats.

Gastrointestinal: Occasional mild epigastric discomfort, substantially improved from presentation. Bowel habits have normalized from baseline. Mild bloating present. Denies nausea at rest, vomiting, melena, or hematochezia. Stools are formed and of normal color. No recurrence of jaundice or scleral icterus.

Neurological: Reports persistent bilateral fingertip and toe paresthesias — numbness and tingling, worse in fingertips than toes. She rates this at 3/10 severity and notes it has improved from its worst point. No burning pain. She is able to perform most activities of daily living but notes some difficulty with fine motor tasks such as fastening small buttons.

Dermatologic: No ongoing alopecia-related concerns. Minimal residual hair thinning. No rash or skin breakdown.

Endocrine/Metabolic: Diet-controlled type 2 diabetes — blood glucose levels have been variable during treatment with a trend toward higher fasting sugars (ranging 115-160 mg/dL fasting per home glucose log). She is not yet on pharmacologic therapy but her PCP is monitoring closely. History of hypertension, well-controlled on amlodipine.

Musculoskeletal: Reports generalized deconditioning and mild bilateral lower extremity weakness, attributed to fatigue and decreased activity during chemotherapy.

Pulmonary/Cardiac: No dyspnea, cough, or chest pain.

Genitourinary: Denies urinary symptoms. No dysuria, hematuria, or frequency changes.

Psychosocial: The patient reports moderate anxiety related to uncertainty about surgical eligibility and next steps. She has been engaged with the patient navigator and social work. She remains highly motivated to pursue curative-intent therapy. She expressed that she understands this process is lengthy and she is prepared to continue.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, pancreatic head and uncinate process — cT3 N1 M0, Stage IIB (AJCC 8th edition); currently on neoadjuvant treatment protocol
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diet-controlled; blood glucose trending upward during chemotherapy
3. Hypertension — well-controlled
4. Biliary obstruction secondary to pancreatic head mass — treated with FCSEMS placement
5. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) — bilateral distal symmetric, primarily oxaliplatin-related, grade 3 at nadir, improved to grade 1-2 on treatment break

PAST SURGICAL / PROCEDURAL HISTORY
- ERCP with fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent (FCSEMS) placement — uncomplicated, performed prior to initiation of neoadjuvant chemotherapy
- EUS-guided fine needle biopsy of pancreatic head mass — confirmed PDAC

MEDICATIONS
1. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
2. Ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours as needed (nausea — PRN, currently not using regularly)
3. Prochlorperazine 10 mg orally every 6 hours as needed (nausea/vomiting — PRN backup)
4. Loperamide 2 mg as needed for diarrhea (PRN)
5. Pegfilgrastim (Neulasta) 6 mg subcutaneously — given with each chemotherapy cycle; last dose given with cycle 8
6. Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg as needed for discomfort (OTC, per patient report)
7. Multivitamin (OTC)

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

Social History: Retired librarian. Of Ghanaian descent. 15-pack-year smoking history, quit 10 years prior. No alcohol or illicit drug use. Lives with her daughter. Has strong family support network. Active in her church community. She has not returned to volunteer work during active treatment but expresses hope to do so.

Family History: No family history of pancreatic cancer. No known BRCA1/2 mutations in the family. No other known hereditary cancer syndromes.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Temperature 98.4°F, heart rate 74 bpm, blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, weight 138 lbs (down from 160 lbs at initial presentation, reflecting ~22 lbs total weight loss over the course of her illness and treatment; up approximately 1.5 lbs from her nadir 3 weeks ago), height 5'4", BMI 23.7 kg/m2.

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-appearing, mildly fatigued-appearing Ghanaian woman in no acute distress. Sitting comfortably in the exam chair with her daughter beside her.

HEENT: No scleral icterus — biliary stent is functional. Mucous membranes moist. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis or thrush. Mild diffuse hair thinning noted, no complete alopecia.

Lymph Nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema. Good peripheral pulses bilaterally.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild residual epigastric tenderness on deep palpation, no guarding or rigidity. No palpable hepatomegaly (liver edge not appreciated below the right costal margin). Spleen not palpable. No ascites appreciated on exam. Active bowel sounds in all quadrants. The prior ERCP port site is well healed.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in upper and lower extremities proximally. Decreased sensation to light touch and vibration in the distal fingertips bilaterally, more pronounced on the right. Decreased sensation to light touch in the bilateral toes. Romberg mildly positive. Gait is steady on a flat surface but mildly cautious. Deep tendon reflexes 1+ at the ankles bilaterally (diminished but present), 2+ at the knees and brachioradialis bilaterally.

Skin/Extremities: Mild bilateral nail changes (Beau's lines) consistent with chemotherapy effect. No hand-foot syndrome. No rash. Mild bilateral lower extremity muscle atrophy consistent with deconditioning.

MSK: No joint effusions. No bony tenderness.

---

LABORATORY DATA (Obtained within 1 week of this visit)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.8 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 10.4 g/dL (mild anemia, stable from nadir; trend is improving)
- Hematocrit: 31.2%
- Platelets: 224 x10^9/L (recovered from nadir cytopenias)
- ANC: 3.1 x10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL (normalized with biliary stent in place)
- Direct bilirubin: 0.3 mg/dL
- Alkaline phosphatase: 112 U/L (normalized from 420 at presentation)
- AST: 32 U/L
- ALT: 28 U/L
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2)
- Glucose: 148 mg/dL (non-fasting; elevated, consistent with worsening diabetes control)
- Albumin: 3.2 g/dL (mildly low, improved from nadir; nutritional deficiency likely multifactorial)
- Total protein: 6.4 g/dL
- Sodium 138, Potassium 3.8, Chloride 102, Bicarbonate 24 (all within normal limits)

Tumor Markers:
- CA 19-9: 85 U/mL (declined 93% from peak of 1,204 U/mL at diagnosis; notable downtrend throughout chemotherapy: 1,204 → 580 (after cycle 2) → 310 (after cycle 4) → 85 (post cycle 8))
- CEA: 3.1 ng/mL (borderline, up from 2.8 ng/mL at last visit but below baseline of 7.1 ng/mL; will monitor)

Coagulation Studies (obtained in preparation for potential surgical planning):
- PT/INR: 1.1
- PTT: 28 seconds

HbA1c: 7.4% (elevated, reflecting worsening glycemic control; to be communicated to PCP)

---

IMAGING SUMMARY (Most Recent Restaging CT, approximately 3 weeks prior to this visit)

CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol contrast (reviewed in detail at today's tumor board):
- Primary tumor: 2.6 cm residual mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate process, down from 3.8 cm at baseline. The mass is hypoenhancing and maintains contact with the SMA but the arc of abutment has clearly decreased — the tumor board radiologist estimated abutment at less than 90 degrees on today's review, consistent with a meaningful reduction from the baseline 170-180 degrees.
- SMA: Abutment is estimated at less than 90 degrees. No encasement. Fat plane between the mass and the SMA lateral wall is partially reconstituted.
- SMV/portal confluence: The SMV appears patent with no significant residual narrowing at this time. The focal narrowing that was previously appreciated has resolved.
- Pancreatic duct: Residual upstream dilation of the main pancreatic duct to approximately 5.5 mm (decreased from 7 mm).
- Common bile duct: Patent stent in place; no proximal biliary dilation. CBD not separately evaluable posterior to the stent.
- Peripancreatic lymph nodes: No clearly enlarged lymph nodes above 1 cm. The previously noted 0.9 cm peripancreatic node is not clearly visualized on current imaging.
- Liver: No focal hepatic lesions. No biliary dilation.
- Peritoneum/ascites: No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity.
- Lungs (lung bases): No nodules or masses. No pleural effusions.
- Overall impression: Favorable response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy with reduction in primary tumor size and significant improvement in the degree of vascular involvement. No evidence of distant metastatic disease.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. Borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), pancreatic head and uncinate process — favorable response to neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX/FOLFIRI.

Mrs. Osei-Bonsu has had a meaningful response to 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Her CA 19-9 has declined 93% from a pre-treatment peak of 1,204 U/mL to 85 U/mL. Cross-sectional imaging demonstrates a 32% reduction in maximum tumor diameter (3.8 → 2.6 cm) and a substantial reduction in SMA abutment from approximately 170-180 degrees to under 90 degrees, with restoration of SMV patency. This degree of radiographic and serologic response is highly encouraging in the setting of borderline resectable disease. There is no evidence of distant metastatic disease on the most recent restaging CT. She has no evidence of disease progression.

2. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) — improving on treatment break, now approximately grade 1-2.

Her neuropathy was the primary driver of oxaliplatin discontinuation after cycle 6. With approximately 4 weeks off all neurotoxic chemotherapy, she has recovered to an estimated grade 1-2 level. Ongoing monitoring is important, as she may receive additional neurotoxic agents (capecitabine as a radiosensitizer has modest neurotoxicity).

3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — worsening glycemic control in the setting of cancer-related stress, chemotherapy effect, and possibly early pancreatic exocrine/endocrine dysfunction.

HbA1c 7.4%, fasting blood glucose trending in the 115-160 range. She is currently diet-controlled only. Will communicate findings to her PCP with a recommendation for close glucose monitoring, particularly in anticipation of chemoradiation which may further affect appetite, nutrition, and glucose control. If glucose control worsens, pharmacologic intervention (metformin or basal insulin) will be needed, especially ahead of a potential Whipple procedure which carries known risk of new-onset or worsened diabetes.

4. Mild anemia — stable, likely multifactorial (chemotherapy-related myelosuppression, nutritional, possible chronic disease).

Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL. Improving from nadir. No transfusion requirement at this time. Iron studies and B12/folate to be checked if not already done recently.

5. Nutritional status — ongoing concern; albumin mildly depressed at 3.2 g/dL.

Patient has lost approximately 22 lbs total from pre-diagnosis weight. Albumin 3.2 g/dL. She will be referred to our pancreatic oncology dietitian for intensive nutritional optimization during the window between completing chemotherapy and initiation of chemoradiation, and in the peri-surgical period.

---

PLAN

Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Consensus — Today's Discussion and Recommendation:

The case was reviewed in the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board today. All available cross-sectional imaging (baseline CT, MRI/MRCP, restaging CTs after 4 cycles and after 8 cycles) was presented by our abdominal radiologist. The pathology (original EUS biopsy — moderately differentiated PDAC, CK7+/CK19+/MUC1+) was reviewed. The clinical trajectory (CA 19-9 from 1,204 → 85 U/mL; tumor reduction from 3.8 cm → 2.6 cm; SMA abutment from ~175° → <90°; SMV now patent) was summarized. The patient's current performance status (ECOG 1), neuropathy recovery, nutritional status, and comorbidities (diabetes, hypertension) were reviewed.

Board consensus recommendation:

1. Consolidation chemoradiation prior to surgical resection:
   The board unanimously recommended consolidation chemoradiation as the next treatment step, with the goals of further sterilizing the surgical margin (particularly the retroperitoneal/SMA margin, which remains of concern given the history of abutment), addressing potential micrometastatic residual disease in the surgical field, and maximizing the likelihood of an R0 resection. The addition of pre-operative chemoradiation in this setting is supported by several institutional experiences and trial data, including the role of radiation in improving locoregional control in borderline resectable disease. Given the degree of response to chemotherapy and the restored SMV patency, the board felt the patient now has a reasonable chance at an R0 Whipple.

   Radiation oncology is planning:
   - IMRT (intensity-modulated radiation therapy) with CBCT-based daily image guidance
   - Treatment to the primary tumor with appropriate margin, with coverage of the celiac axis nodal region and peripancreatic lymph nodes
   - Concurrent capecitabine (Xeloda) as a radiosensitizer at standard dosing (825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days)
   - Radiation oncology will perform simulation and planning in the next 1-2 weeks; treatment to begin approximately 3 weeks from today

   The patient will be seen by radiation oncology for a formal consultation (see separate note when available).

2. Reassessment of surgical candidacy approximately 4-6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation:
   Following chemoradiation, repeat pancreatic protocol CT will be obtained to assess for response and confirm absence of distant metastatic disease before proceeding to surgery. Hepatobiliary surgery has reviewed the case and agrees to plan for open pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) pending continued radiographic eligibility at that time. They noted the SMV involvement will likely require careful dissection and possible venous resection/reconstruction, and the family has been informed of this possibility.

3. Monitoring and supportive care in the interim period:
   - Nutritional optimization: Referral to pancreatic oncology dietitian. High-calorie, high-protein diet with possible oral nutritional supplements. Target weight stabilization prior to chemoradiation. Patient counseled to aim for 25-30 kcal/kg/day. Pancreatic enzymes may be considered if exocrine insufficiency symptoms develop (pale greasy stools, bloating).
   - Neuropathy: Continue to monitor. If grade worsens during chemoradiation (capecitabine has modest potential), dose modification of capecitabine may be needed. Duloxetine 30 mg daily offered to patient for symptomatic relief of neuropathic discomfort; patient agreed to a trial.
   - Glucose monitoring: Communicate to PCP that HbA1c is 7.4% and fasting glucoses are trending upward. Recommend close monitoring and initiation of pharmacologic therapy if fasting glucose consistently exceeds 180 mg/dL, particularly in anticipation of peri-operative period. Patient referred back to PCP for diabetes management optimization prior to surgery.
   - Anemia: Recheck CBC in 3 weeks (prior to chemoradiation simulation visit). Obtain iron studies and B12/folate. If hemoglobin drops below 9.0 g/dL or she becomes symptomatic, may consider IV iron or erythropoiesis-stimulating agent cautiously given oncologic context.
   - CA 19-9 and CEA: Will recheck at radiation oncology simulation visit and again prior to surgery to track trajectory.
   - Hypertension: Continue amlodipine. Blood pressure well controlled today.

4. Genomic testing and germline counseling:
   Germline BRCA1/2 testing has been recommended given the importance of identifying hereditary risk; this is pending or has been ordered — to be confirmed with patient and primary care. Somatic NGS on the diagnostic biopsy specimen has been ordered and results are expected. These results will be important for understanding tumor biology and potential therapeutic vulnerability (e.g., PARP inhibitor eligibility for BRCA-mutant disease, PD-L1 status for immunotherapy context).

5. Goals of care and psychosocial support:
   Mrs. Osei-Bonsu was counseled extensively today, with her daughter present. We discussed the very encouraging response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy and the recommendation to proceed with chemoradiation and then surgery with curative intent. We acknowledged the significant journey she has already undergone and expressed that the treatment plan ahead is still substantial. The patient expressed understanding, gratitude, and continued motivation to pursue surgery. She was tearful briefly when discussing the possibility that surgery might not be feasible if new metastatic disease were to appear on restaging, but appeared to process this with equanimity and a strong support system. She was reassured that we will be with her at every step.

   Palliative care and social work remain available for ongoing support. She is connected with the patient navigator. She was given written materials summarizing the chemoradiation process and what to expect.

6. Follow-up:
   - Radiation oncology consultation within the next week
   - Radiation simulation: approximately 2 weeks
   - Initiation of chemoradiation: approximately 3 weeks from today
   - Return to medical oncology clinic mid-chemoradiation for toxicity assessment and/or at completion of chemoradiation
   - Post-chemoradiation restaging CT: approximately 5 weeks after radiation completion
   - Surgical oncology for pre-operative evaluation: following post-chemoradiation restaging
   - Return to medical oncology post-surgery (if resection performed) for discussion of adjuvant therapy

---

Time spent in direct patient care and coordination of multidisciplinary discussion: 75 minutes (including 35-minute tumor board review, 30-minute patient encounter, and 10 minutes of documentation and care coordination).

All questions from the patient and her daughter were answered. Patient voiced understanding of the plan and agreement to proceed.

[Electronically signed — Attending Medical Oncologist, GI/Pancreatic Oncology Program]
